name: Create Reusable Terraform Modules
description: Build, structure, and publish reusable Terraform modules with inputs, outputs, and proper documentation.

steps.title: Create the module directory structure
steps.description: Set up the standard file layout for a Terraform module with variables, outputs, and main configuration.

steps.title: Define input variables
steps.description: Declare configurable inputs with types, defaults, descriptions, and validation rules.

steps.title: Write the main resource definitions
steps.description: Implement the core resources that the module creates and manages.
steps.tip: Use the 'this' naming convention for the primary resource in a single-resource module.

steps.title: Define module outputs
steps.description: Export values that consumers of the module will need for connecting other resources.

steps.title: Call the module from root configuration
steps.description: Use the module in your root configuration by referencing the local path and passing variables.

steps.title: Validate and test the module
steps.description: Initialize, validate, and plan the configuration to ensure the module works correctly.
steps.command: terraform init && terraform validate && terraform plan
steps.warning: Always run validate before plan to catch syntax and type errors early.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Terraform Module Scaffolding Script
# Creates a well-structured module with all required files

MODULE_NAME="${1:?Usage: $0 <module-name>}"
MODULE_DIR="modules/${MODULE_NAME}"

echo "==> Creating module: ${MODULE_NAME}"
mkdir -p "${MODULE_DIR}"

cat > "${MODULE_DIR}/versions.tf" <<'EOF'
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.5.0"
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}
EOF

cat > "${MODULE_DIR}/variables.tf" <<'EOF'
variable "environment" {
  type        = string
  description = "Environment name"
}

variable "tags" {
  type        = map(string)
  description = "Additional tags to apply"
  default     = {}
}
EOF

cat > "${MODULE_DIR}/main.tf" <<EOF
# Main resources for ${MODULE_NAME} module
# TODO: Add your resource definitions here
EOF

cat > "${MODULE_DIR}/outputs.tf" <<EOF
# Outputs for ${MODULE_NAME} module
# TODO: Export values for consumers
EOF

cat > "${MODULE_DIR}/README.md" <<EOF
# ${MODULE_NAME} Module

## Usage

\`\`\`hcl
module "${MODULE_NAME}" {
  source      = "./modules/${MODULE_NAME}"
  environment = "dev"
}
\`\`\`

## Inputs

| Name | Description | Type | Default |
|------|-------------|------|---------|
| environment | Environment name | string | - |
| tags | Additional tags | map(string) | {} |

## Outputs

(Add outputs here)
EOF

echo "==> Module scaffolded at ${MODULE_DIR}/"
ls -la "${MODULE_DIR}/"
echo ""
echo "Next steps:"
echo "  1. Edit ${MODULE_DIR}/main.tf to add resources"
echo "  2. Edit ${MODULE_DIR}/variables.tf to add inputs"
echo "  3. Edit ${MODULE_DIR}/outputs.tf to export values"
echo "  4. Run: terraform init && terraform validate"

faq.question: Should I pin provider versions inside modules?
faq.answer: Define required_providers in the module's versions.tf with a version constraint, but let the root module lock the exact version. Use pessimistic constraints like ~> 5.0.

faq.question: How do I version a module for consumers?
faq.answer: Use Git tags (v1.0.0, v1.1.0) and reference them in the source URL: source = "git::https://github.com/org/module.git?ref=v1.0.0". For Terraform Registry, publish with semantic versioning.

faq.question: What is the difference between a module and a root configuration?
faq.answer: A root configuration is what you run terraform apply on. A module is a reusable package of resources called from a root configuration or other modules. Every Terraform config is technically a module.
